topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET616 - APPLIED EARTH SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weathering – relevance, influence of and on earth systems, types and controlling factors; soil – formation and controls, soil profile, soil erosion and conservation methods; fluvial processes – hydrological cycle, fluvial erosion, transportation and deposition, fluvial landforms; stages of stream development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Weathering – Definition & Relevance

**Weathering** is the in-situ disintegration, decomposition, and alteration of rocks and minerals at or near the Earth's surface through physical, chemical, and biological processes, without involving significant transport of the weathered material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** Weathering is the response of earth materials to the conditions prevailing at the interface between the lithosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere. It is a **preparatory process** for erosion, soil formation, and landscape evolution.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of weathering as the Earth's natural "aging" process — just like a metal gate left in the rain slowly rusts, peels, and weakens over the years, rocks exposed to sun, rain, wind, roots, and microbes gradually crack, dissolve, and crumble where they sit. The gate doesn't move — it just disintegrates in place. That is weathering. The rust flakes are then carried away by wind and water — that is **erosion**.

### Relevance to Earth Systems
| Earth System | Influence of Weathering |
|---|---|
| Lithosphere | Breaks down parent rock into regolith; creates loose debris available for erosion |
| Hydrosphere | Dissolves minerals → regulates river water chemistry (e.g., $Ca^{2+}$, $HCO_3^{-}$) |
| Atmosphere | Consumes $CO_2$ via chemical weathering → long-term climate regulation (carbon sink) |
| Biosphere | Releases nutrients (P, K, Ca) → sustains ecosystems; soil formation enables plant growth |

## 1.2 Soil – Definition

**Soil** is the thin, biologically active uppermost layer of the Earth's crust, composed of mineral matter, organic matter, water, and air, formed through pedogenesis (soil-forming processes) acting on weathered parent material over time.

> [!NOTE]
> A pedologist (soil scientist) calls true soil **"pedosphere"** — it is the interface where rock, air, water, and life meet.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a slice of bread left on a table. Over time, the top dries and cracks, mold (biology) appears, crumbs fall, and the bread slowly merges with the table surface. Soil forms in a similar layered, time-dependent manner — each "layer" records a chapter in its formation history.

## 1.3 Fluvial Processes – Definition

**Fluvial processes** refer to all geomorphic activities carried out by running water (streams and rivers) including **erosion, transportation, and deposition** of sediments, which collectively shape valleys, floodplains, deltas, and a wide range of landforms.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture a garden hose left running on a sand pile. The water cuts a tiny channel (erosion), carries sand grains down-slope (transportation), and drops them where the flow slows (deposition). Now scale that hose up to a Himalayan river — and you have the Ganges carving the Himalayas and building the Sundarbans delta.

## 1.4 Stages of Stream Development

A stream's life cycle is described in three stages:

1. **Youthful (Youth) Stage** – steep gradient, V-shaped valleys, active downcutting, waterfalls, rapids.
2. **Mature Stage** – moderate gradient, meanders begin, floodplains develop, lateral erosion dominant.
3. **Old (Senescent) Stage** – very low gradient, broad meanders, oxbow lakes, floodplains, eventual base-level conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Stream development stages follow **William Morris Davis's "Geographical Cycle" (1899)** — often called the **Cycle of Erosion**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Weathering

### A. Physical (Mechanical) Weathering
Disintegration of rock **without chemical change**. Breaks large masses into smaller fragments, increasing surface area for chemical attack.

| Process | Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Frost wedging | Water freezes in cracks → expands ~9% → fractures rock | Himalayan granite tors |
| Thermal expansion | Diurnal heating/cooling → differential expansion → exfoliation | Deccan basalt domes |
| Exfoliation | Unloading of overburden → parallel fractures peel off | Yosemite Half Dome |
| Salt crystallization | Salts grow in pores → crystal pressure disintegrates rock | Coastal Kerala laterite |
| Root wedging | Plant roots grow in joints → physical splitting | Tree roots in rock crevices |

### B. Chemical Weathering
Decomposition of rock by chemical reactions; primary agents are water, oxygen, $CO_2$, and organic acids.

| Process | Reaction | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Solution / Dissolution | $CaCO_3 + H_2O + CO_2 \rightarrow Ca(HCO_3)_2$ | Karst caves (limestone terrain) |
| Oxidation | $4Fe + 3O_2 \rightarrow 2Fe_2O_3$ | Red-brown rust stains (Kerala laterite) |
| Hydration | $CaSO_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O$ | Anhydrite → Gypsum expansion |
| Hydrolysis | $2KAlSi_3O_8 + 2H^+ + 9H_2O \rightarrow Al_2Si_2O_5(OH)_4 + 2K^+ + 4H_4SiO_4$ | Feldspar → Kaolinite (clay) |
| Carbonation | $CO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2CO_3$ | Acidifies water, attacks limestone |

### C. Biological Weathering
- **Direct:** Root penetration, lichen/moss acid secretion, burrowing organisms.
- **Indirect:** Organic acids (humic, fulvic) accelerate hydrolysis; microbial respiration releases $CO_2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Penck's Weathering Rule:** In tropical humid climates, chemical weathering dominates; in arid/polar climates, physical weathering dominates. This is a high-frequency KTU concept.

## 2.2 Controlling Factors of Weathering

| Factor | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Climate** (temp + rainfall) | Most powerful control; warm + wet = intense chemical; cold/dry = physical |
| Rock type & mineralogy | Limestone → solution; Granite → hydrolysis; Basalt → oxidation |
| Vegetation cover | Supplies organic acids + root pressure |
| Slope & exposure | Steep slopes → rapid removal of debris → fresh exposure |
| Time | Long exposure → deep weathering profiles (laterite, saprolite) |
| Tectonic activity | Creates fractures → increases surface area for weathering |

## 2.3 Soil Formation (Pedogenesis) – Factors & Controls

Soil is formed by the interaction of five factors, formalized by **Hans Jenny (1941)** as:

$$S = f(Cl, O, R, P, T)$$

Where:
- $S$ = Soil
- $Cl$ = Climate
- $O$ = Organisms (biota)
- $R$ = Relief (topography)
- $P$ = Parent material
- $T$ = Time

### Soil-Forming Processes
1. **Eluviation** – Removal of soluble + fine particles from upper horizons (E horizon).
2. **Illuviation** – Deposition of eluviated material in lower horizons (B horizon).
3. **Humification** – Decomposition of organic matter → humus.
4. **Mineralization** – Conversion of organic N, P, S to inorganic forms.
5. **Calcification / Podzolization / Laterization** – Climate-specific pedogenic regimes.

## 2.4 Soil Profile

A vertical cross-section of soil from surface to bedrock shows distinct **horizons** designated **O–A–E–B–C–R**.

| Horizon | Description | Key Process |
|---|---|---|
| **O** | Organic layer (litter, humus) | Accumulation of organic matter |
| **A** | Topsoil – dark, mineral + humus rich | Eluviation, biological mixing |
| **E** | Eluvial – leached, light colored | Loss of clay, Fe, Al, organic matter |
| **B** | Subsoil – zone of accumulation | Illuviation of clay, oxides, carbonates |
| **C** | Weathered parent material (regolith) | In-situ weathering of rock |
| **R** | Bedrock (unweathered) | Parent lithology |

## 2.5 Soil Erosion – Types and Mechanisms

| Type | Definition | Agent |
|---|---|---|
| Sheet erosion | Uniform removal of thin soil layers by runoff | Overland flow |
| Rill erosion | Formation of small, shallow channels (rills) | Concentrated runoff |
| Gully erosion | Deep channels incised by runoff; cannot be erased by tillage | Heavy runoff |
| Wind erosion | Detachment + transport of soil by wind | Wind in arid/semi-arid regions |
| Streambank erosion | Removal of soil from banks of rivers | Fluvial action |

### Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE) – Wischmeier & Smith (1978)

$$A = R \times K \times LS \times C \times P$$

Where:
- $A$ = Predicted soil loss (tons/ha/yr)
- $R$ = Rainfall-runoff erosivity factor
- $K$ = Soil erodibility factor (tons·hr/ha·MJ·mm)
- $LS$ = Slope length and steepness factor (dimensionless)
- $C$ = Cover management factor (dimensionless)
- $P$ = Conservation practice factor (dimensionless)

## 2.6 Soil Conservation Methods

| Method | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Contour ploughing | Ploughing along contour lines reduces runoff velocity |
| Terracing | Step-like fields on slopes reduce gradient |
| Strip cropping | Alternating strips of crops to slow runoff |
| Cover crops / mulching | Protect surface from raindrop impact |
| Afforestation | Permanent vegetation cover binds soil |
| Check dams / gabions | Control gully erosion in channels |
| Crop rotation | Maintains soil structure and fertility |
| Windbreaks / shelterbelts | Reduce wind erosion |

## 2.7 The Hydrological Cycle

The **hydrological cycle** is the continuous movement of water through the Earth system driven by solar energy and gravity. Key processes: **Evaporation, Transpiration, Condensation, Precipitation, Infiltration, Runoff, Sub-surface flow**.

### Water Balance Equation

$$P = ET + R + I + \Delta S$$

Where:
- $P$ = Precipitation
- $ET$ = Evapotranspiration
- $R$ = Surface runoff
- $I$ = Infiltration (deep percolation)
- $\Delta S$ = Change in storage

### Runoff Estimation (Rational Method)

$$Q = C \times I \times A$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Peak runoff (m³/s)
- $C$ = Runoff coefficient (0 to 1)
- $I$ = Rainfall intensity (mm/hr or m/s)
- $A$ = Catchment area (m² or hectares)

## 2.8 Fluvial Erosion

Three sub-processes:
1. **Hydraulic action** – Direct force of water dislodging loose particles.
2. **Abrasion (corrasion)** – Sediment-laden water scrapes channel bed and banks.
3. **Attrition** – Particles collide and break into smaller fragments during transport.
4. **Solution (corrosion)** – Chemical dissolution of soluble rocks.

## 2.9 Stream Discharge (Manning's Equation)

$$Q = \frac{1}{n} \times A \times R_h^{2/3} \times S^{1/2}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Discharge (m³/s)
- $n$ = Manning's roughness coefficient
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of flow (m²)
- $R_h$ = Hydraulic radius = $A / P$ (m), with $P$ = wetted perimeter
- $S$ = Bed slope (m/m)

## 2.10 Sediment Transportation

Sediment moves by:
- **Suspension** – fine particles (clay, silt) carried in water column
- **Saltation** – sand grains bouncing along bed
- **Traction (bedload)** – gravel rolled/dragged along bed
- **Solution load (dissolved)** – ions in solution

### Hjulström Curve Concept
Critical erosion velocity is highest for medium sand (~0.2 mm); finer particles are cohesive and harder to erode despite low settling velocity.

## 2.11 Fluvial Landforms (Erosional and Depositional)

| Erosional Landform | Depositional Landform |
|---|---|
| V-shaped valley | Floodplain |
| Gorge / Canyon | Alluvial fan |
| Waterfall | Delta |
| Pothole | Natural levee |
| River terrace | Meander, Oxbow lake |
| Plunge pool | Point bar, Slip-off slope |
| Truncated spur | braided channel deposits |

## 2.12 Stages of Stream Development (Davis's Cycle)

| Stage | Gradient | Valley shape | Dominant process |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Youth** | Steep | V-shaped, narrow, deep | Vertical (downcutting) erosion |
| **Mature** | Moderate | Wider valley, meanders appear | Lateral erosion + mass movement |
| **Old** | Gentle | Broad floodplain, meanders, oxbows | Deposition dominant, base-level reached |

**Base level** = the lowest level to which a stream can erode its bed. Ultimate base level = **sea level**; local base level = lakes, resistant rock outcrops, reservoirs.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Manning's Equation (Continuity + Chezy + Empirical slope correction)

### Step 1: Continuity of Flow

The discharge through any open channel cross-section is the product of area and mean velocity:

$$Q = A \times V$$

This is a kinematic statement — whatever volume enters per second must exit per second for steady flow.

### Step 2: Chezy's Mean Velocity Formula (1775)

For uniform flow in an open channel, Chezy proposed that mean velocity depends on hydraulic radius and energy slope:

$$V = C \times \sqrt{R_h \times S}$$

Here, $C$ is the Chezy coefficient (m¹/²/s), depending on channel roughness.

### Step 3: Combine Continuity and Chezy

Substituting Chezy's mean velocity into the continuity equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= A \times V \\
  &= A \times C \times \sqrt{R_h \times S} \\
  &= C \times A \times R_h^{1/2} \times S^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Manning's Empirical Replacement of Chezy's C (1889)

Robert Manning (Irish engineer) experimentally found that for rough turbulent flow:

$$C = \frac{R_h^{1/6}}{n}$$

where $n$ is the **Gauckler–Manning coefficient** (roughness). Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= A \times \left( \frac{R_h^{1/6}}{n} \right) \times R_h^{1/2} \times S^{1/2} \\
  &= \frac{1}{n} \times A \times R_h^{1/6 + 1/2} \times S^{1/2} \\
  &= \frac{1}{n} \times A \times R_h^{2/3} \times S^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Manning–Gauckler–Strickler equation**, valid for SI units when $n$ is in $s/m^{1/3}$.

### Step 5: Engineering Utility

Used to:
- Design open channels, drains, spillways.
- Predict flood discharges.
- Estimate sediment transport capacity.
- Model river behavior in hydraulic software (HEC-RAS).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example – Manning's Discharge

**Problem:** A rectangular channel is 4 m wide, water depth is 2 m, bed slope $S = 0.0008$, Manning's $n = 0.025$. Find the discharge.

### Step 1: Cross-sectional Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= b \times d \\
  &= 4 \text{ m} \times 2 \text{ m} \\
  &= 8 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Wetted Perimeter (rectangular channel)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= b + 2d \\
  &= 4 + 2(2) \\
  &= 8 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Hydraulic Radius

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_h &= \frac{A}{P} \\
    &= \frac{8}{8} \\
    &= 1 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Manning's Equation

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \frac{1}{n} \times A \times R_h^{2/3} \times S^{1/2} \\
  &= \frac{1}{0.025} \times 8 \times (1)^{2/3} \times (0.0008)^{1/2} \\
  &= 40 \times 8 \times 1 \times 0.02828 \\
  &= 9.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $Q \approx 9.05 \, m^3/s$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example – USLE Soil Loss Calculation

**Problem:** A small agricultural catchment in Kerala has the following parameters:
- $R = 250$ (MJ·mm/ha·hr·yr)
- $K = 0.32$ (tons·hr/ha·MJ·mm)
- $LS = 1.5$
- $C = 0.20$ (sparse cover)
- $P = 1.0$ (no conservation practice)

Find predicted annual soil loss.

### Step 1: Substitute into USLE

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= R \times K \times LS \times C \times P \\
  &= 250 \times 0.32 \times 1.5 \times 0.20 \times 1.0
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Multiply Sequentially

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 250 \times 0.32 \times 1.5 \times 0.20 \\
  &= 80 \times 1.5 \times 0.20 \\
  &= 120 \times 0.20 \\
  &= 24 \text{ tons/ha/yr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** Annual soil loss = **24 tons/ha/yr**, which is high. Recommended: introduce terracing to lower $P$ factor and increase cover to reduce $C$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A tolerable soil loss (T value) for most agricultural soils is **5–11 tons/ha/yr**. Anything above this is unsustainable.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example – Rational Method Runoff

**Problem:** A 5-hectare catchment in a sub-urban area receives rainfall at intensity 50 mm/hr. Runoff coefficient $C = 0.35$. Find peak runoff.

### Step 1: Convert units
- $A = 5$ hectares = $5 \times 10^4 \, m^2$
- $I = 50$ mm/hr = $50 / 3600 / 1000 \, m/s = 1.389 \times 10^{-5} \, m/s$

### Step 2: Apply Rational Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= C \times I \times A \\
  &= 0.35 \times 1.389 \times 10^{-5} \times 5 \times 10^4 \\
  &= 0.35 \times 0.6944 \\
  &= 0.243 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $Q \approx 0.243 \, m^3/s$ ≈ 243 L/s.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix – Weathering vs. Climate (Penck–Peltier Belt)

| Climatic Zone | Dominant Weathering | Typical Result |
|---|---|---|
| Polar / Periglacial | Frost wedging, glacial | Angular debris, talus |
| Temperate (humid) | Moderate chemical + biological | Thick brown forest soils |
| Tropical humid (Kerala) | Strong chemical, laterization | Laterite, bauxite, deep red soils |
| Arid (Rajasthan) | Thermal, salt, weak chemical | Desert pavement, calcrete |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Weathering Types – Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Weathering of Rocks] --> B[Physical Weathering]
    A --> C[Chemical Weathering]
    A --> D[Biological Weathering]
    B --> B1[Frost Wedging]
    B --> B2[Thermal Expansion]
    B --> B3[Exfoliation]
    B --> B4[Salt Crystallization]
    C --> C1[Solution / Dissolution]
    C --> C2[Oxidation]
    C --> C3[Hydration]
    C --> C4[Hydrolysis]
    C --> C5[Carbonation]
    D --> D1[Root Wedging]
    D --> D2[Organic Acid Secretion]
    D --> D3[Burrowing Organisms]
```

## 4.2 Soil Profile – Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O[O Horizon - Organic Litter] --> A[A Horizon - Topsoil]
    A --> E[E Horizon - Eluvial Leached]
    E --> B[B Horizon - Subsoil Illuvial]
    B --> C[C Horizon - Weathered Regolith]
    C --> R[R Horizon - Bedrock]
```

## 4.3 Hydrological Cycle – Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OCEAN[Ocean] -->|Evaporation| ATMOS[Atmosphere]
    ATMOS -->|Condensation| CLOUD[Cloud Formation]
    CLOUD -->|Precipitation| LAND[Land Surface]
    LAND -->|Infiltration| SUBSURF[Groundwater]
    LAND -->|Runoff| RIVER[Rivers]
    RIVER --> OCEAN
    LAND -->|Evapotranspiration| ATMOS
    SUBSURF -->|Baseflow| RIVER
```

## 4.4 Fluvial Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FP[Fluvial Processes] --> ER[Erosion]
    FP --> TR[Transportation]
    FP --> DP[Deposition]
    ER --> ER1[Hydraulic Action]
    ER --> ER2[Abrasion Corrasion]
    ER --> ER3[Attrition]
    ER --> ER4[Solution Corrosion]
    TR --> TR1[Suspension]
    TR --> TR2[Saltation]
    TR --> TR3[Traction Bedload]
    TR --> TR4[Dissolved Load]
    DP --> DP1[Alluvial Fan]
    DP --> DP2[Floodplain]
    DP --> DP3[Delta]
    DP --> DP4[Meander Oxbow]
```

## 4.5 Stages of Stream Development – Davis's Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Y[Youth Stage] -->|Continued Erosion| M[Mature Stage]
    M -->|Base Level Approach| O[Old Stage]
    Y --> Y1[Steep Gradient]
    Y --> Y2[V Shaped Valley]
    Y --> Y3[Waterfalls Rapids]
    M --> M1[Moderate Gradient]
    M --> M2[Meanders Begin]
    M --> M3[Floodplain Forms]
    O --> O1[Gentle Gradient]
    O --> O2[Broad Meanders]
    O --> O3[Oxbow Lakes Deltas]
```

## 4.6 Fluvial Landforms – Erosion vs Deposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LF[Fluvial Landforms] --> EL[Erosional Landforms]
    LF --> DL[Depositional Landforms]
    EL --> EL1[V Shaped Valley]
    EL --> EL2[Gorge Canyon]
    EL --> EL3[Waterfall Plunge Pool]
    EL --> EL4[River Terraces]
    DL --> DL1[Alluvial Fan]
    DL --> DL2[Natural Levee]
    DL --> DL3[Point Bar]
    DL --> DL4[Oxbow Lake]
    DL --> DL5[Delta]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Differentiate between physical and chemical weathering. List the controlling factors of weathering.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

| Aspect | Physical Weathering | Chemical Weathering |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Disintegration without chemical change | Decomposition by chemical reactions |
| Agents | Temperature, frost, salt, pressure | Water, oxygen, $CO_2$, organic acids |
| Products | Smaller fragments of same mineral | New minerals (clays, oxides) |
| Climate | Cold / arid / high altitude | Hot / humid / tropical |
| Example | Frost wedging in Himalayas | Laterization in Kerala |

**Controlling factors of weathering:**
1. **Climate** (most dominant) – temperature and rainfall
2. **Rock type and mineralogy** – solubility and mineral stability
3. **Vegetation** – supplies organic acids and root pressure
4. **Topography / slope** – affects exposure and debris removal
5. **Time** – duration of exposure
6. **Tectonic activity** – creates fractures and exposes fresh surfaces

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**What is a soil profile? Briefly describe the major horizons.**

**Model Answer:**

A **soil profile** is a vertical section through the soil from the surface down to the parent bedrock, exposing distinct layers called **horizons** that differ in physical, chemical, and biological properties. The major horizons (top to bottom) are:

1. **O Horizon** – Organic layer of decomposing litter; dark in color; rich in humus.
2. **A Horizon (Topsoil)** – Mixed mineral + organic matter; site of intense biological activity; subject to eluviation.
3. **E Horizon** – Zone of leaching; light-colored; depleted of clay, Fe, Al.
4. **B Horizon (Subsoil)** – Zone of accumulation (illuviation); enriched in clay, oxides, carbonates.
5. **C Horizon** – Partially weathered parent material (regolith); little biological activity.
6. **R Horizon** – Unweathered consolidated bedrock.

A typical mature soil in humid tropical Kerala shows a deep O–A–B–C sequence with prominent laterite in the B horizon.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three main types of weathering with suitable examples. Discuss the role of climate as a controlling factor.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE). What are the major methods of soil conservation adopted in India?

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) – 7 Marks**

1. **Physical (Mechanical) Weathering (2 marks):**
   - Disintegration of rock into smaller fragments **without chemical change**.
   - Increases surface area, exposing more rock to chemical attack.
   - Examples: Frost wedging in Himalayas (water freezes in cracks); thermal expansion in deserts (diurnal heating/cooling); exfoliation in granite domes (unloading of overburden); salt crystallization in coastal Kerala.

2. **Chemical Weathering (2.5 marks):**
   - Decomposition of rocks by chemical reactions.
   - Major processes:
     - **Solution:** $CaCO_3 + H_2O + CO_2 \rightarrow Ca(HCO_3)_2$ – karst cave formation in limestone.
     - **Oxidation:** $4Fe + 3O_2 \rightarrow 2Fe_2O_3$ – rust staining in laterite.
     - **Hydrolysis:** Feldspar + water → Kaolinite + ions.
     - **Carbonation:** $CO_2 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2CO_3$ – acidifies water, attacks carbonate rocks.

3. **Biological Weathering (1.5 marks):**
   - Roots penetrate joints and physically split rock.
   - Lichens and microbes secrete organic acids (humic, fulvic) that accelerate hydrolysis.
   - Burrowing animals (earthworms, termites) mix soil and expose fresh surfaces.

4. **Climate as Controlling Factor (1 mark):**
   - **Hot + humid** (Kerala) → chemical + biological dominate → laterite soils.
   - **Cold + dry** (Ladakh) → frost wedging dominates.
   - **Hot + dry** (Rajasthan) → thermal expansion, salt crystallization.
   - Climate is the most dominant factor because it controls temperature, moisture, and biological activity.

**Part (b) – 7 Marks**

**Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE) – 4 marks:**

$$A = R \times K \times LS \times C \times P$$

| Factor | Meaning | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| R | Rainfall erosivity | 50–800 (MJ·mm/ha·hr·yr) |
| K | Soil erodibility | 0.02–0.69 (tons·hr/ha·MJ·mm) |
| LS | Slope length + steepness | 0–10+ |
| C | Cover management | 0 (dense forest) to 1 (bare) |
| P | Conservation practice | 0 (terraced) to 1 (none) |

**Soil Conservation Methods in India (3 marks):**
1. **Contour ploughing** – along contour lines on slopes.
2. **Terracing** – bench terraces in hilly regions (tea/coffee estates of Munnar).
3. **Strip cropping** – alternating strips of different crops.
4. **Afforestation** – planting trees on degraded land (e.g., Chipko movement).
5. **Check dams / Gabion structures** – control gully erosion.
6. **Shelterbelts** – reduce wind erosion in arid regions (e.g., Indira Gandhi Canal area).
7. **Cover crops and mulching** – reduce raindrop impact.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. In Part (a), students often **confuse carbonation with carbonization** — carbonation is dissolution by carbonic acid; do not mix it with industrial carbonization.
> 2. In Part (b), many students **forget to mention the units of USLE factors** or treat $C$ and $P$ as the same. They are distinct: $C$ = vegetation cover, $P$ = engineering conservation measure.
> 3. Marks are often lost for **not naming specific Indian examples** (e.g., laterite in Kerala, terracing in Munnar).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the hydrological cycle with a neat sketch. Write the water balance equation and explain each term.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the three stages of stream development as proposed by W.M. Davis. List any six fluvial landforms with one-line descriptions.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) – 7 Marks**

**Hydrological Cycle (5 marks):**

The hydrological cycle is the continuous circulation of water between the oceans, atmosphere, and land, powered by solar energy and gravity. The major processes are:

1. **Evaporation** – water from oceans, lakes, and rivers turns into vapor.
2. **Transpiration** – water released by plants through stomata.
3. **Condensation** – water vapor cools and forms clouds.
4. **Precipitation** – water returns to Earth as rain, snow, sleet, hail.
5. **Infiltration** – water seeps into the soil to recharge groundwater.
6. **Surface Runoff** – water flows over land to streams, rivers, lakes, and back to the sea.

(Refer to Mermaid diagram 4.3 for the functional flow.)

**Water Balance Equation (2 marks):**

$$P = ET + R + I + \Delta S$$

- $P$ = Precipitation (input)
- $ET$ = Evapotranspiration (loss to atmosphere)
- $R$ = Surface runoff (output to streams)
- $I$ = Infiltration + deep percolation (recharge)
- $\Delta S$ = Change in storage (soil moisture, groundwater, surface reservoirs)

For long-term average, $\Delta S \rightarrow 0$, so $P = ET + R + I$.

**Part (b) – 7 Marks**

**Davis's Cycle of Erosion (4 marks):**

W.M. Davis (1899) proposed that a stream evolves through three stages after tectonic uplift, ending at base level:

1. **Youthful Stage (1.5 marks):**
   - Steep gradient, rapid vertical erosion.
   - V-shaped narrow valleys, waterfalls, rapids.
   - River actively downcuts; minimal floodplain.
   - Example: upper reaches of Ganges in the Himalayas.

2. **Mature Stage (1.5 marks):**
   - Gradient reduced; lateral erosion begins.
   - Meanders form, floodplains develop, tributaries integrated.
   - Valley widens; mass wasting on valley sides.
   - Example: middle Ganges in the Indo-Gangetic plains.

3. **Old (Senescent) Stage (1 mark):**
   - Very gentle gradient, broad floodplains, large meanders, oxbow lakes.
   - Dominant process is deposition; river approaches base level.
   - Deltas form at mouth.
   - Example: lower Ganges in Bangladesh.

**Six Fluvial Landforms (3 marks – 0.5 each):**

1. **V-shaped Valley** – Narrow, deep, formed by active downcutting in youth stage.
2. **Waterfall** – Vertical drop where a hard rock layer overlies softer rock.
3. **Alluvial Fan** – Cone-shaped deposit at the base of a mountain front where a stream loses velocity.
4. **Meander** – Sinuous looping curve of a mature river.
5. **Oxbow Lake** – Crescent-shaped lake formed when a meander loop is cut off.
6. **Delta** – Triangular depositional landform at river mouth where it meets a sea or lake.
7. **Natural Levee** – Low ridges along river banks built up by repeated flooding.
8. **Floodplain** – Flat valley floor adjacent to a river, periodically flooded.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. In Part (a), many students **omit evapotranspiration** and only write evaporation. Always mention transpiration from plants — it is essential.
> 2. In Part (b), students often **misclassify the maturity stage** — a river is "mature" not when it has meanders alone, but when vertical erosion decreases and lateral erosion takes over. Marks are awarded for explaining **process dominance**, not just landform names.
> 3. **Forgetting base level** in the old stage is a common 1-mark loss.
> 4. Naming landforms without one-line descriptions leads to **partial marking** only.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Module 2 — Quick Recall Sheet**

### Weathering
- Weathering = in-situ breakdown; erosion = transport of weathered material.
- Three types: Physical, Chemical, Biological.
- **Climate is the most powerful control** (Penck's rule).
- Frost wedging, exfoliation, oxidation, hydrolysis, carbonation are top-favorite terms.
- Chemical weathering also acts as a **long-term carbon sink** (weathering of silicates consumes atmospheric $CO_2$).

### Soil
- Soil forming factors: $S = f(Cl, O, R, P, T)$ (Jenny's equation, 1941).
- Soil profile: O → A → E → B → C → R.
- Erosion types: Sheet, Rill, Gully, Wind, Streambank.
- USLE: $A = R \times K \times LS \times C \times P$.
- Tolerable soil loss (T): 5–11 t/ha/yr.
- Conservation: contour ploughing, terracing, afforestation, check dams.

### Fluvial Processes & Hydrology
- Hydrological cycle: $P = ET + R + I + \Delta S$.
- Rational method: $Q = C \times I \times A$ (peak runoff, small catchments).
- Manning's equation: $Q = \frac{1}{n} A R_h^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ – applies to open-channel uniform flow.
- Three fluvial actions: Erosion, Transportation, Deposition.
- Transportation modes: Suspension, Saltation, Traction, Solution load.

### Fluvial Landforms
- Erosional: V-valley, Gorge, Waterfall, River terrace, Pothole.
- Depositional: Alluvial fan, Delta, Floodplain, Natural levee, Point bar, Oxbow lake.

### Stages of Stream Development (Davis)
- **Youth:** Steep, V-shaped, vertical erosion, waterfalls.
- **Mature:** Moderate gradient, meanders, lateral erosion, floodplains.
- **Old:** Gentle gradient, broad meanders, oxbows, deposition dominant.
- **Base level:** Sea level (ultimate) or local feature (e.g., lake, dam).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Whenever an ESE question mentions "explain" → expect a **process-based answer with examples**. When it says "describe" → expect **classification with characteristics**. When it says "differentiate" → use a **comparison table**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drainage patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET616 - APPLIED EARTH SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Drainage patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **drainage pattern** is the geometric configuration or spatial arrangement of a river system and its tributaries as viewed on a topographic map, aerial photograph, or satellite image. It represents the integrated response of a landscape to the combined influence of **lithology, structure, relief, climate, and tectonic history** over geological time.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme (OECET616 – Applied Earth Systems), drainage patterns are classified under **geomorphological indicators** of subsurface geology. They are critical in:

- **Hydrogeology** (locating aquifers in fractured/jointed rock)
- **Engineering geology** (site selection for dams, tunnels, and highways)
- **Mineral exploration** (placer deposits, vein deposits)
- **Hazard assessment** (floods, landslides, gully erosion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A drainage pattern is a *visible surface expression* of *invisible subsurface structure*. Wherever rocks of differing resistance to erosion lie side by side, water exploits the weakest paths and the resulting stream network "maps" the geology from above.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Reading the Map of a Fractured Window"

Imagine a **car window that has been struck with a small hammer**. The cracks radiate outward in a web of irregular lines. Now imagine **rainwater** falling on that window. The water will flow *only along the cracks*, never across unbroken glass. The pattern of trickling water (the "drainage") becomes a perfect blueprint of the crack network (the "geology").

> The Earth's crust behaves the same way. Streams are like rainwater, and joints/faults/lithological contacts are the "cracks." The drainage pattern, therefore, **literally traces the structural grain of the underlying rock**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for First-Time Learners:** Drainage patterns are not random. Each pattern has a name (dendritic, trellis, radial, etc.) and a specific geological cause. A trained geologist can glance at a drainage map and infer the rock type, dip direction, and structural history of an unseen terrain.

### 1.3 The Six First-Order Controls on Drainage Patterns

| # | Control Factor | Mechanism of Influence |
|---|----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Lithology** (rock type) | Homogeneous rocks → dendritic; alternating weak/strong rocks → trellis |
| 2 | **Structure** (folds, faults, joints) | Tectonic features guide stream orientation |
| 3 | **Slope / Relief** | Steep slopes → parallel patterns; flat plains → meandering |
| 4 | **Climate** | Arid → internal drainage; humid → through-flowing |
| 5 | **Tectonic history** | Recent uplift rejuvenates streams and resets patterns |
| 6 | **Base level changes** | Sea-level fall → incision; rise → aggradation |

The formal expression of basin size and texture is given by **drainage density (D)**, a dimensionless parameter defined as the total stream length per unit basin area:

$$D = \frac{\sum L_i}{A}$$

where $\sum L_i$ is the total length of all streams in the basin (in **km**) and $A$ is the basin area (in **km²**). The standard unit is **km⁻¹**, with typical values ranging from **< 0.5 km⁻¹** (resistant rock, low relief) to **> 3.0 km⁻¹** (weak rock, high relief).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Drainage Patterns — Operational Logic

Drainage patterns are classified into two broad families:

1. **Sequential patterns** — develop in a *new, undeformed* landscape reflecting original slope and lithology.
2. **Insequent patterns** — develop where the geology imposes a structural control *regardless* of original slope.

Below, each major pattern is broken down by **geometry → geological cause → typical setting**.

#### 2.1.1 Dendritic Pattern (Tree-like)

- **Geometry:** Resembles the branching of an oak or maple tree. Tributaries join the main stream at acute angles, typically **< 90°**, with no preferred orientation.
- **Geological Cause:** Develops on **homogeneous, horizontally bedded, or massive crystalline rocks** (e.g., granite, basalt, sandstone) that have **uniform resistance to erosion**.
- **Typical Setting:** Stable cratons, sedimentary plateaus, deeply weathered shields.
- **Engineering Note:** Indicates structurally uncomplicated ground — generally favorable for foundations, *but* the homogeneity means poor groundwater localization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** "Dendritic" comes from the Greek *dendron* (tree). If the question asks what drainage pattern forms on granite or massive sandstone, the answer is **dendritic**.

#### 2.1.2 Trellis Pattern (Garden Lattice)

- **Geometry:** Long, parallel main streams joined at **near-right angles (≈ 90°)** by short, straight tributaries. Resembles a garden trellis or vine.
- **Geological Cause:** Develops on **gently dipping alternating bands of resistant and weak sedimentary rocks** (e.g., sandstone–shale alternations in a homoclinal sequence). Main streams flow **along strike valleys** in soft rock; tributaries flow **down dip** along joints.
- **Typical Setting:** Folded and tilted sedimentary belts, the classical Appalachian-style belts.
- **Engineering Note:** The rectangular junctions indicate **strong joint control** — useful for locating water wells, but also **tunnel hazard zones** where joints concentrate.

#### 2.1.3 Radial Pattern (Spokes of a Wheel)

- **Geometry:** Streams flow *outward* from a central high point like the spokes of a wheel.
- **Geological Cause:** Develops on **domes, volcanic cones, batholithic intrusions, or erosional remnants** where the ground slopes in all directions from a single apex.
- **Typical Setting:** Volcanoes (e.g., Mt. Kilimanjaro), salt domes, laccoliths.
- **Engineering Note:** Excellent natural water-shed; reservoirs constructed on radial domes have predictable catchments.

#### 2.1.4 Centripetal (Internal Drainage) Pattern

- **Geometry:** Streams flow *inward* toward a central low point (lake, playa, or salt pan).
- **Geological Cause:** Develops in **structural depressions, calderas, volcanic craters, or arid basins** with no external outlet.
- **Typical Setting:** Endorheic basins, Lake Chad, Great Basin of the western USA.
- **Engineering Note:** Indicates **closed basins** — water does not exit; salts accumulate → saline/alkaline soils.

#### 2.1.5 Rectangular Pattern

- **Geometry:** Tributaries join main streams at **right angles**, with **short, straight segments and sharp bends**. Similar to trellis, but tributaries are *not* parallel to each other.
- **Geological Cause:** Strong control by **orthogonal joint sets or fault systems** (e.g., N–S and E–W fractures).
- **Typical Setting:** Jointed granites, basalt flows, brittle deformation zones.
- **Engineering Note:** Rectangular patterns in a project area should trigger **detailed lineament mapping** — they often mark permeable fracture zones suitable for bore wells.

#### 2.1.6 Annular Pattern (Ring-shaped)

- **Geometry:** A series of **concentric circular** streams wrapping around a central high. Main streams follow ring-shaped weak-rock outcrops; tributaries cut radially across resistant ridges.
- **Geological Cause:** Develops on **deeply eroded domes** where alternating resistant (quartzite, sandstone) and weak (shale) layers are exposed as concentric rings.
- **Typical Setting:** Eroded structural domes (e.g., the Ozark Dome, USA).
- **Engineering Note:** Rare and diagnostic — instantly reveals a domal structure.

#### 2.1.7 Parallel Pattern

- **Geometry:** Tributaries are **parallel to each other** and join the main stream at **acute angles**. Limited branching.
- **Geological Cause:** Develops on **uniform, steeply dipping slopes** or **eroded fault scarps** with moderate to high relief.
- **Typical Setting:** Coastal ranges, fault-block mountains, dip slopes of hogbacks.
- **Engineering Note:** Indicates **uniform, steeply dipping strata** — useful for tracing resistant beds along strike.

#### 2.1.8 Deranged Pattern

- **Geometry:** **Chaotic, non-integrated**, with swamps, lakes, and disorganized streams.
- **Geological Cause:** **Recent glaciation** has obliterated pre-existing drainage. The landscape has not yet re-established equilibrium.
- **Typical Setting:** Recently deglaciated terrain (Canada, Scandinavia).
- **Engineering Note:** Poor for dams — high leakage risk through glacial deposits.

#### 2.1.9 Contorted (Barbed) Pattern

- **Geometry:** Tributaries enter the main stream in a **direction opposite to the general slope** (like fishhook barbs).
- **Geological Cause:** **Stream piracy (capture)** or **reversal of drainage** due to tectonic tilting or glacial diversion.
- **Typical Setting:** Mature mountain belts, regions of past glaciation.
- **Engineering Note:** Marks ancient drainage reversals — important in **paleohydrology** and **tectonic reconstruction**.

### 2.2 Horton's Laws of Drainage Composition (Quantitative Foundation)

Robert E. Horton (1945) established three empirical laws that quantify drainage networks. These are routinely tested in KTU examinations.

**Law 1 — Stream Number Law:**
The number of streams of successive orders follows a *geometric* series.

$$N_u = R_B^{\,(\Omega - u)}$$

where:
- $N_u$ = number of streams of order $u$
- $R_B$ = bifurcation ratio (dimensionless)
- $\Omega$ = order of the main stream (highest order)

**Law 2 — Stream Length Law:**
The average length of streams of successive orders follows a *geometric* series.

$$\overline{L}_u = \overline{L}_1 \cdot R_L^{\,(u-1)}$$

where $R_L$ is the **length ratio** and $\overline{L}_1$ is the mean length of first-order streams.

**Law 3 — Stream Slope Law:**
The average slope of streams decreases geometrically with increasing order.

$$\overline{S}_u = \overline{S}_1 \cdot R_S^{\,(u-1)}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Typical Range | Geological Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------|---------------|---------------------|
| Stream Order (Strahler) | $u$ | Hierarchical integer (1 = fingertip) | 1 to 7+ | Position in the network |
| Bifurcation Ratio | $R_B$ | $R_B = N_u / N_{u+1}$ | 3.0 – 5.0 (normal) | Shape & dissection |
| Length Ratio | $R_L$ | $R_L = \overline{L}_{u+1} / \overline{L}_u$ | 1.5 – 3.0 | Persistence of streams |
| Drainage Density | $D$ | $D = \sum L_i / A$ | 0.5 – 3.0 km⁻¹ | Texture / infiltration |
| Stream Frequency | $F_s$ | $F_s = \sum N_u / A$ | 1 – 10 km⁻² | Number per area |
| Texture Ratio | $T$ | $T = N_1 / P$ | varies | Fineness of dissection |
| Elongation Ratio | $R_e$ | $R_e = D_c / L_b$ | 0.6 – 1.0 | Basin shape |
| Circularity Ratio | $R_c$ | $R_c = 4\pi A / P^2$ | 0.4 – 0.8 | Basin compactness |
| Form Factor | $F_f$ | $F_f = A / L_b^{\,2}$ | 0.1 – 0.8 | Hydrological response |
| Ruggedness Number | $R_n$ | $R_n = D \times H$ | 0.1 – 1.0 | Relief × density |

> **Note on units:** $A$ = basin area (km²), $P$ = basin perimeter (km), $L_b$ = basin length (km), $D_c$ = diameter of circle of same area as basin (km), $H$ = total relief (km), $N_1$ = number of 1st-order streams.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule for Exams:** In the bifurcation ratio formula $N_u / N_{u+1}$, the *smaller* $u$ (e.g., 1st order) is always the **numerator**, and the *larger* $u$ is the **denominator**. Getting this inverted is the most common KTU answer-sheet mistake.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Drainage Pattern Analysis |
|-------|------------------------------------------|
| **Civil Engineering** | Site selection for dams, tunnels, bridges; identifying potentially unstable ground |
| **Mining Engineering** | Tracing placer deposits, locating reef/fault-controlled ore bodies |
| **Hydrogeology** | Drilling bore wells along lineament-intersections in rectangular patterns |
| **Environmental Engineering** | Delineating flood-prone zones, planning stormwater networks |
| **Urban Planning** | Mapping natural drainage before urbanization; designing retention ponds |
| **Disaster Management** | Predicting landslide-prone slopes, gully erosion zones |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Horton's Bifurcation Ratio and its Geological Meaning

The bifurcation ratio $R_B$ is the foundation of quantitative drainage analysis. Let us derive its meaning step-by-step.

**Step 1 — Define stream order using Strahler's rule.**

Every fingertip tributary is labelled order **1**. Where two streams of order $u$ meet, the downstream segment becomes order $u+1$. When a lower-order stream joins a higher-order stream, the order does not change.

> *Example:* If two 1st-order streams join → order 2. If a 1st-order joins a 2nd-order → remains 2. If two 2nd-order streams join → order 3.

**Step 2 — Count streams of each order from a topographic map.**

Suppose a basin has:
- $N_1 = 50$ first-order streams
- $N_2 = 12$ second-order streams
- $N_3 = 4$ third-order streams
- $N_4 = 1$ fourth-order stream (the main stem)

**Step 3 — Compute the bifurcation ratio for each successive pair.**

$$R_{B(1/2)} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{50}{12} = 4.17$$

$$R_{B(2/3)} = \frac{N_2}{N_3} = \frac{12}{4} = 3.00$$

$$R_{B(3/4)} = \frac{N_3}{N_4} = \frac{4}{1} = 4.00$$

**Step 4 — Compute the weighted mean bifurcation ratio.**

$$\overline{R}_B = \frac{N_1 + N_2 + N_3 + \cdots}{N_2 + N_3 + N_4 + \cdots}$$

Substituting values:

$$\overline{R}_B = \frac{50 + 12 + 4}{12 + 4 + 1} = \frac{66}{17} = 3.88$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

A value of $\overline{R}_B \approx 3.88$ lies within the **normal range of 3.0 – 5.0**, which indicates:
- A **moderately dissected** terrain
- **Low structural control** (since high $R_B$ often reflects homogeneous lithology)
- **Typical humid-temperate** climate influence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geological Diagnosis from $R_B$:**
> - $R_B < 3$ → strongly elongated basin, mountainous, high relief
> - $R_B = 3$ to $5$ → normal mature basin
> - $R_B > 5$ → flat, low-relief basin, perhaps in arid climate or with strong structural control

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Drainage Density Calculation

**Problem:** A river basin of area **320 km²** contains the following stream segments:

| Order | Number of Streams | Average Length (km) |
|-------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1     | 80                | 0.9                 |
| 2     | 20                | 2.5                 |
| 3     | 5                 | 6.0                 |
| 4     | 1                 | 14.0                |

**Step 1 — Compute the total stream length.**

$$\sum L_i = (80 \times 0.9) + (20 \times 2.5) + (5 \times 6.0) + (1 \times 14.0)$$

$$\sum L_i = 72.0 + 50.0 + 30.0 + 14.0 = 166.0 \; \text{km}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the drainage density formula.**

$$D = \frac{\sum L_i}{A} = \frac{166.0 \; \text{km}}{320 \; \text{km}^2}$$

$$D = 0.519 \; \text{km}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**

A drainage density of **0.52 km⁻¹** is **low**, indicating:
- **Resistant bedrock** or
- **High infiltration** (permeable surface, dense vegetation) or
- **Low relief**

The basin is likely to have **low peak runoff** and **high base flow**, suggesting it may be useful for **sustained-yield water supply** rather than hydropower.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Length Ratio and the Geometric Series Check

**Step 1 — Define length ratio.**

$$R_L = \frac{\overline{L}_{u+1}}{\overline{L}_u}$$

**Step 2 — Verify Horton's law geometric progression.**

Taking the data from Section 3.2:

$$R_{L(1/2)} = \frac{2.5}{0.9} = 2.78$$

$$R_{L(2/3)} = \frac{6.0}{2.5} = 2.40$$

$$R_{L(3/4)} = \frac{14.0}{6.0} = 2.33$$

**Step 3 — Compute the weighted mean.**

$$\overline{R}_L = \frac{0.9 \times 2.78 + 2.5 \times 2.40 + 6.0 \times 2.33}{0.9 + 2.5 + 6.0}$$

$$\overline{R}_L = \frac{2.50 + 6.00 + 14.00}{9.4} = \frac{22.50}{9.4} = 2.39$$

**Step 4 — Test geometric progression (KTU favourite).**

For an ideal Horton network, lengths should follow $L_u = L_1 \cdot R_L^{(u-1)}$. Using $L_1 = 0.9$ and $R_L = 2.39$:

$$\overline{L}_2 \;(\text{predicted}) = 0.9 \times 2.39^{1} = 2.15 \; \text{km} \quad (\text{observed: } 2.5)$$

$$\overline{L}_3 \;(\text{predicted}) = 0.9 \times 2.39^{2} = 5.14 \; \text{km} \quad (\text{observed: } 6.0)$$

$$\overline{L}_4 \;(\text{predicted}) = 0.9 \times 2.39^{3} = 12.29 \; \text{km} \quad (\text{observed: } 14.0)$$

The **deviations are small (< 25%)**, confirming a **well-developed Horton network** in approximately geometric progression.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How to Read a Drainage Map

Use this decision matrix in the exam to identify any drainage pattern from a sketch or description:

| Diagnostic Question | If "Yes" → Pattern |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Tributaries branch randomly at acute angles? | **Dendritic** |
| Tributaries join main stream at ~90°, parallel to each other? | **Trellis** |
| Streams flow *outward* from a central high? | **Radial** |
| Streams flow *inward* into a closed depression? | **Centripetal** |
| Right-angle junctions *with* non-parallel tributaries? | **Rectangular** |
| Concentric ring-shaped streams around a dome? | **Annular** |
| Tributaries are parallel to each other? | **Parallel** |
| Chaotic, lake-filled, poorly integrated? | **Deranged** |
| Tributaries pointing *upstream*? | **Barbed (Contorted)** |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Drainage Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Observe Drainage Map] --> B{Is there a central high or low point?}
    B -- Central HIGH --> C{Streams radiate outward?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Radial Pattern - Dome or Volcano]
    C -- No --> E{Streams form concentric rings?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Annular Pattern - Eroded Dome]
    E -- No --> G[Re-examine: possibly centripetal]
    B -- Central LOW --> H[Centripetal Pattern - Closed Basin]
    B -- No central point --> I{Is junction angle near 90 degrees?}
    I -- Yes --> J{Are tributaries parallel to each other?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Trellis Pattern - Folded Sedimentary Rocks]
    J -- No --> L[Rectangular Pattern - Jointed or Faulted Rock]
    I -- No --> M{Is the pattern tree-like with random angles?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Dendritic Pattern - Homogeneous Rock]
    M -- No --> O{Are tributaries parallel to main stream?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Parallel Pattern - Steep Uniform Slope]
    O -- No --> Q{Is drainage chaotic with lakes and swamps?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Deranged Pattern - Glaciated Terrain]
    Q -- No --> S{Do tributaries point upstream?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Barbed Pattern - Stream Piracy]
    S -- No --> U[Re-examine or Complex Mixed Pattern]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How a Drainage Pattern Forms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Subsurface Geology] --> B[Rock Resistance Contrast]
    C[Tectonic Activity] --> B
    D[Climate and Rainfall] --> E[Surface Runoff Pathways]
    B --> E
    F[Initial Slope of Land] --> E
    E --> G[Erosion and Headward Growth]
    G --> H[Stream Network Geometry]
    H --> I[Observed Drainage Pattern]
    I --> J{Geologist Interprets}
    J --> K[Lithology Map]
    J --> L[Structural Map]
    J --> M[Groundwater Potential Zones]
    J --> N[Engineering Hazard Map]
```

**Reading the architecture:**

1. **Subsurface geology** (rock type, dip, fold, fault) and **tectonic activity** combine to create differential resistance to erosion.
2. **Climate and rainfall** determine the volume and energy of running water; **initial slope** sets the gravitational gradient.
3. Surface runoff exploits the weakest paths — joints, faults, soft beds.
4. Through **headward erosion** and **stream capture**, the geometric pattern emerges.
5. The visible pattern becomes a **map of the invisible geology**, which the geologist then "reads" to derive lithological, structural, hydrogeological, and engineering-hazard information.

### 4.3 Schematic of Strahler Stream Ordering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1a[1st order] --> J1
    S1b[1st order] --> J1
    J1[Confluence] --> S2[2nd order]
    S1c[1st order] --> S2
    S2 --> J2[Confluence]
    S2b[2nd order] --> J2
    J2 --> S3[3rd order]
    S1d[1st order] --> S3
    S3 --> J3[Confluence]
    S3b[3rd order] --> J3
    J3 --> S4[4th order MAIN STEM]
```

**Rule reminders encoded in the schematic:**

- Two 1st-order streams meeting → produce a 2nd-order stream.
- A 1st-order joining a 2nd-order → the 2nd-order continues unchanged.
- Two 2nd-order streams meeting → produce a 3rd-order stream.
- This hierarchy is the **basis of all quantitative Horton analysis**.

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Bifurcation Ratio in a Dendritic Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    O4[Order 4 - 1 stream] --> O3a[Order 3 - 3 streams]
    O3a --> O2a[Order 2 - 9 streams]
    O2a --> O1a[Order 1 - 27 streams]
    O3b[Order 3] --> O2b[Order 2]
    O2c[Order 2] --> O1b[Order 1]
    O1c[Order 1] --> O2d[Order 2]
    O1d[Order 1] --> O2d
    O2d --> O3c[Order 3]
    O3c --> O4
```

**Reading the diagram:** In this example $N_1 = 27$, $N_2 = 9$, $N_3 = 3$, $N_4 = 1$, giving $R_B = 3$ at every level — a **perfectly self-similar** dendritic network. This is the textbook ideal.
