# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a cyclone. List the three main categories of cyclones with a distinguishing feature of each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A cyclone is a large-scale low-pressure atmospheric system with winds blowing inward toward a central low-pressure region, rotating counter-clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere.

**Three categories:**
1. **Tropical Cyclones** — Form over warm tropical oceans (SST ≥ 26.5 °C); fueled by latent heat release; have a well-defined eye.
2. **Extra-Tropical Cyclones** — Form along the polar front in mid-latitudes; fueled by horizontal temperature contrast; have cold and warm fronts.
3. **Mesocyclones/Tornadoes** — Small-scale (km scale) violent vortices embedded in thunderstorms; strongest winds on Earth.

---

### **Q2. What is a jet stream? Name the two main jet streams and state where they are located.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A jet stream is a narrow, fast-flowing ribbon of air in the upper troposphere near the tropopause, with wind speeds typically 120–400 km/h, flowing generally west to east.

**Two main jet streams:**
1. **Polar Jet Stream** — Located at **30°–60°** latitude, altitude **9–12 km**, strongest in winter.
2. **Subtropical Jet Stream** — Located at approximately **30°** latitude, altitude **12–14 km**, persistent throughout the year.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the structure of a tropical cyclone with a neat diagram. Describe the role of latent heat in its formation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Structure (Outside → Inside):**
1. **Outflow Layer (12–15 km):** Air diverges outward at the top, evacuating mass from the storm column.
2. **Eyewall:** A ring of intense thunderstorms surrounding the eye, where the strongest winds and heaviest rain occur. Vertical wind speeds can exceed 250 km/h.
3. **Eye (30–65 km):** A calm, cloud-free central region. Air descends (subsidence) and warms adiabatically. Central pressure is the lowest — sometimes below 900 hPa in Category 5 cyclones.
4. **Spiral Rainbands:** Curved bands of thunderstorms spiraling inward, feeding the eyewall with moisture and angular momentum.
5. **Inflow Layer (0–3 km):** Warm, moist air flows inward across the ocean surface, supplying energy.

**Role of Latent Heat in Formation:**

- Warm ocean water (SST ≥ 26.5 °C) evaporates rapidly.
- Water vapor rises and condenses in the eyewall thunderstorms.
- Condensation releases **latent heat of vaporization** (~2.5 × 10⁶ J/kg).
- This heat warms the central column of the storm.
- Warmed air column expands, lowering the surface pressure further (per the **hypsometric equation**).
- Lower surface pressure intensifies the pressure gradient, which accelerates winds inward.
- Stronger winds enhance evaporation, completing a positive feedback loop (**WISHE mechanism — Wind-Induced Surface Heat Exchange**).

**Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 (Mermaid block).

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Derive the thermal wind relation and explain how it explains the existence of jet streams.**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:** (Refer to Section 3.1 for full derivation.)

Starting from hydrostatic balance and geostrophic wind:

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = -\rho g, \quad u_g = -\frac{1}{\rho f}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}
$$

Differentiating $u_g$ with respect to $z$ and applying the ideal gas law yields:

$$
\frac{\partial u_g}{\partial z} = -\frac{g}{fT}\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}
$$

This is the **thermal wind relation**.

**Explanation of Jet Streams:**

- The equator is warm; the poles are cold. So $\partial T / \partial y < 0$ (temperature decreases northward).
- In the Northern Hemisphere, $f > 0$, so $\partial u_g / \partial z > 0$ — westerly winds **increase with height**.
- This increase continues until the **tropopause**, where the strong static stability caps further vertical growth.
- Therefore, **maximum westerly wind speed occurs at the tropopause**, forming the **polar jet stream**.
- The **subtropical jet** similarly forms where the Hadley cell subsidence meets mid-latitude westerlies.

**Conclusion:** The thermal wind relation is the fundamental physical reason jet streams exist. Steeper equator-to-pole temperature contrast (winter) → stronger jet stream.

---

### **Question B** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks] Differentiate between tropical and extra-tropical cyclones. Explain why jet streams steer extra-tropical cyclones.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Tropical Cyclone | Extra-Tropical Cyclone |
|---|---|---|
| Latitude | 5°–25° | 30°–70° |
| Energy Source | Latent heat (warm ocean) | Baroclinic instability (temperature gradient) |
| Structure | Eye + spiral bands | Cold front + warm front (no eye) |
| Size | 200–2000 km | 1000–4000 km |
| Season | Summer/Autumn | Winter/Spring |
| Coriolis | Weak (low latitude) | Strong (mid-latitude) |

**Why Jet Streams Steer Extra-Tropical Cyclones:**

- Jet streams are concentrated corridors of high-speed air in the upper troposphere.
- Extra-tropical cyclones form and travel beneath the jet stream, embedded in the **polar front jet**.
- The jet's high wind speeds create strong **vertical wind shear**, which by **thermal wind balance** corresponds to a strong **horizontal temperature gradient** at the surface.
- This temperature gradient is the energy source of the cyclone.
- The **upper-level divergence** ahead of a jet stream trough (southward dip) enhances surface convergence, deepening the cyclone.
- Therefore, the cyclone tracks along the jet stream path, generally eastward, following the **Rossby wave** pattern.

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Define the gradient wind. Derive the gradient wind equation for a cyclonic (low-pressure) circulation and discuss its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The gradient wind is the wind that balances the **pressure gradient force**, the **Coriolis force**, and the **centrifugal force** in steady, horizontal, frictionless flow along curved isobars.

**Derivation:**

In natural coordinates, with radius of curvature $R$, the radial equation of motion for steady flow is:

$$
\frac{V^2}{R} + fV = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial r}
$$

Rearranging as a quadratic in $V$:

$$
\frac{1}{R}V^2 + fV + \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
V = -\frac{fR}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{fR}{2}\right)^2 - \frac{R}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial r}}
$$

(The **+√** root is selected; the −√ root is non-physical for cyclones.)

**Physical Significance:**

- For a **cyclone** (low pressure), $\partial p / \partial r < 0$, so the term under the square root exceeds $(fR/2)^2$. The gradient wind is **sub-geostrophic** ($V_{\text{gradient}} < V_{\text{geostrophic}}$) because centrifugal force partially offsets the pressure gradient.
- For an **anticyclone** (high pressure), $\partial p / \partial r > 0$, so $V_{\text{gradient}} > V_{\text{geostrophic}}$ — **super-geostrophic**.
- In **tropical cyclones** where $f$ is small, the Coriolis term is weak and **centrifugal balance dominates** → this is called **cyclostrophic flow**.
- The gradient wind is a more accurate model than the geostrophic wind for systems with strong curvature (cyclones, anticyclones), making it essential for accurate cyclone intensity forecasting.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Coriolis force direction:** In the Northern Hemisphere, deflection is to the **right** of motion. In the Southern Hemisphere, to the **left**. Getting this wrong loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the eye in cyclone diagrams:** The eye is a defining feature — never omit it in a "structure of tropical cyclone" question.
> 3. **Mixing up geostrophic and gradient winds:** Geostrophic = straight flow (no curvature). Gradient = curved flow (cyclones, anticyclones). Examiners WILL check this distinction.
> 4. **Forgetting the minus sign in thermal wind:** Many students drop the sign. $\partial u_g / \partial z = -g/(fT) \cdot \partial T/\partial y$ — sign matters.
> 5. **Not stating the season:** "Polar jet streams are strongest in winter" — examiners expect this explicitly.
> 6. **Using "Coriolis effect" when "Coriolis force" is asked:** They are related but distinct. Be precise.
> 7. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Always include m/s, km/h, hPa, °C — partial credit depends on it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### **Cyclones — Rapid Revision**
- A **cyclone** is a low-pressure system with inward-spiraling winds.
- **Counter-clockwise** in Northern Hemisphere; **clockwise** in Southern Hemisphere.
- **Tropical cyclones** need: SST ≥ 26.5 °C, latitude ≥ 5°, low wind shear, mid-level moisture.
- **Structure:** Outflow → Eyewall → Eye → Spiral bands → Inflow.
- **Energy:** Latent heat release (positive feedback via WISHE).
- **Naming:** Hurricane (Atlantic), Typhoon (W. Pacific), Cyclone (Indian Ocean).
- **Saffir-Simpson Scale:** Cat 1 (119 km/h) to Cat 5 (≥ 252 km/h).

### **Jet Streams — Rapid Revision**
- A **jet stream** is a fast (120–400 km/h) ribbon of westerly wind in the upper troposphere.
- Located at the **tropopause break**, typically **9–12 km** altitude.
- **Polar jet** (30°–60°): strongest in winter; **subtropical jet** (~30°): persistent.
- **Thermal wind relation:** $\partial u_g / \partial z = -\frac{g}{fT}\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}$ — vertical shear ∝ temperature gradient.
- **Rossby waves:** Large meanders (ridges & troughs) of the jet.
- Steers weather systems, including extra-tropical cyclones.
- **Tropical easterly jet** (Indian summer monsoon) flows east to west — a critical India-specific exam point.

### **Key Equations at a Glance**
- $f = 2\Omega\sin\phi$, where $\Omega = 7.29 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s.
- $u_g = -\frac{1}{\rho f}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}$.
- $V_{\text{gradient}} = -\frac{fR}{2} + \sqrt{(fR/2)^2 - \frac{R}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial r}}$.
- Tropical cyclone threshold: **SST ≥ 26.5 °C**, depth ≥ 50 m, latitude ≥ 5° from equator.

### **Engineer's Perspective — Why This Matters**
- **Aviation** saves fuel and time using jet streams.
- **Disaster management** uses cyclone + jet stream models to save lives.
- **Renewable energy** explores jet stream turbines.
- **Climate science** links jet stream behavior to extreme weather (heatwaves, cold spells, floods).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 5 KTU-Favorite One-Liners to Memorize:**
> 1. *"Tropical cyclones rotate counter-clockwise in the Northern Hemisphere due to the Coriolis effect."*
> 2. *"Jet streams are strongest in winter because the equator-to-pole temperature gradient is maximum."*
> 3. *"The thermal wind relation explains why the strongest winds in the atmosphere occur near the tropopause."*
> 4. *"Extra-tropical cyclones are steered by jet streams along the polar front."*
> 5. *"In a tropical cyclone, the eye is the calmest region but the surrounding eyewall is the most violent."*
